talk_title: The Genomic Landscape of Likely Human Pre-cancers
MOA_summary: Comprehensive genomic profiling of ~1,500 human pre-cancer lesions across 33 tissue types identified high heterogeneity in mutational burden, copy-number alterations, and driver gene alterations, and a random forest model using TMB, ploidy, and genome aberration predicts malignant potential.
key_findings: Pre-cancer samples exhibited coding mutation counts ranging from <10 to >3,000 across tissue types.
43% of pre-cancers lacked detectable driver gene alterations compared with 11% in TCGA cancers.
Tumor mutational burden, ploidy, and percent genome aberrant were the top three features distinguishing cancer-like from precancer-like samples.
In Barrett's esophagus, the cancer-like score correlated with progression to invasive cancer.
open_questions: How to collect longitudinal clinical outcome data for indolent pre-cancers that never progress?
What is the role of germline predisposition in pre-cancer genomic signatures?

talk_title: Mechanisms of Tumor Dormancy Through Immune Niche Formation and Its Impact on Immune Therapeutic Relapse

models.name: Tgp‑myeloid knockout mouse with inducible GFP tumor cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: TGF‑βR2 knockout in myeloid cells, disseminated tumor cells

MOA_summary: Dormant tumor cells up‑regulate CD200, which engages CD200R on NK cells within a monocyte‑neutrophil‑NK cell niche, suppressing NK‑mediated killing; CD200 knockdown reduces dormant lesions, and anti‑CD200 therapy eradicates residual dormant cells after chemotherapy, preventing relapse.
key_findings: Dormant lesions (1‑8 cells) were enriched for monocytes, neutrophils, dendritic cells, and NK cells, but lacked T cells.
CD200 expression was high in dormant tumor cells and mediated resistance to NK‑cell killing.
CD200 knockdown reduced dormant lesion burden without affecting proliferative lesions.
Anti‑CD200 antibody combined with cisplatin and anti‑PD‑1 eliminated residual dormant cells and prevented tumor regrowth after therapy withdrawal.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Dormant cells evade immune clearance via CD200‑mediated inhibition of NK cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy reduces proliferative tumor bulk, leaving dormant cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Residual dormant cells persist; anti‑CD200 needed for eradication.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade enhances T‑cell activity; combined with anti‑CD200 targets both T‑cell and NK‑cell pathways.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete elimination of dormant lesions and prevention of relapse.

open_questions: Can CD200 blockade improve outcomes in patients with minimal residual disease after standard therapy?
What is the relevance of CD200‑mediated dormancy in other cancer types?

talk_title: Ligandable PNT Domain Establishes ERG as a Druggable Oncogenic Driver in Prostate Cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Wang
speaker.affiliation: University of Michigan / Shanghai Institute of Chemistry

models.name: ERG+ mouse organoid, ERG+ PDX‑derived organoid

MOA_summary: Pbite‑1 binds the PNT domain of ERG with ~28 µM affinity, stabilizing the protein and disrupting PNT‑mediated protein‑protein interactions, leading to down‑regulation of ERG target genes, induction of apoptosis, and inhibition of invasion in ERG‑positive prostate cancer models.
key_findings: Pbite‑1 stabilizes ERG PNT domain in vitro (DSF) and in cells (CETSA) with KD ~28 µM.
Biotinylated Pbite‑1 pulls down full‑length ERG but not PNT‑deleted fragments, confirming domain specificity.
Pbite‑1 selectively reduces viability and induces apoptosis in ERG‑positive cell lines and organoids, sparing ERG‑negative controls.
Treatment suppresses ERG‑regulated transcriptional programs (MYC, AR, etc.) and inhibits invasion in ERG‑overexpressing models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Pbite‑1 shows modest potency (micromolar) and requires further optimization for clinical translation.
open_questions: Can higher‑affinity PNT binders achieve therapeutic efficacy in vivo?
Will targeting the PNT domain affect other ETS family members and cause off‑target effects?

talk_title: Novel Brain‑Penetrant BRAF Inhibitor HSK42360 for BRAF‑Mutant Glioma
MOA_summary: HSK42360 induces an external rotation of the αC‑helix of BRAF, preventing RAF dimerization and thus avoiding paradoxical MAPK activation; it penetrates the brain (Kp,uu = 1.1) and shows potent inhibition of class I‑III BRAF mutants in vitro and tumor regression in intracranial glioma models.
key_findings: HSK42360 does not cause paradoxical MAPK activation in HCT116 cells.
In vitro IC50s against class II (G469A) and class III (G466V) BRAF mutants ranged from 7.6 nM to 101 nM, outperforming dabrafenib.
Brain penetration Kp,uu = 1.1 exceeds the 0.3 threshold for CNS drugs.
In intracranial A375 CDX mice, HSK42360 significantly prolonged survival.
In a clinical cohort (n=23), ORR was 42.1% (high‑grade) and 50% (low‑grade) with durable responses and reduced BRAF‑inhibitor toxicities.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No paradoxical activation observed; activity retained in NRAS‑mutant resistant models.

combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression in class II/III BRAF models.

combination_strategies.rationale: Overcome resistance in CRC models with EGFR activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Tumor control in cetuximab‑plus‑vemurafenib resistant CRC PDX.

open_questions: Will HSK42360 demonstrate efficacy in BRAF‑inhibitor resistant glioma patients?
Can broader tumor types with BRAF mutations benefit from this brain‑penetrant inhibitor?

talk_title: Active RAS Inhibition Intercepts Pancreas Cancer in Mice

models.name: KPC mouse (KRAS G12D + TP53 mutation)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Develops PanIN at 1‑2 mo, tumors at 3‑4 mo

MOA_summary: RAS inhibitors induce apoptosis of KRAS‑mutant PanIN lesions, reducing their number and delaying tumor onset; intermittent metronomic dosing further prolongs latency, demonstrating that early interception of pre‑malignant KRAS‑driven lesions can extend survival independent of adaptive T‑cell immunity.
key_findings: 10‑day RAS inhibition reduced PanIN lesions from ~400 to significantly fewer in 9‑week‑old KPC mice.
RAS inhibition increased cleaved caspase‑3 in PanIN cells, indicating apoptosis without transdifferentiation.
Four weeks of RAS inhibition delayed tumor onset and improved overall survival in KPC mice.
T‑cell depletion did not affect the interception benefit, suggesting a T‑cell‑independent mechanism.
Metronomic weekly RAS inhibition (1 week on/1 week off) delayed tumor onset by >200 days and extended survival by >1 year.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Interception efficacy appears independent of immune surveillance.
open_questions: How to translate PanIN interception to high‑risk human populations?
Will long‑term intermittent RAS inhibition be safe in humans?

talk_title: Phase 1/2 Study of Deruxnaratib with Gemcitabine and Nab‑Paclitaxel in First‑Line Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer

biomarkers.name: KRAS mutant allele fraction (ctDNA)
biomarkers.finding: 96% of patients achieved ≥50% reduction; 61% cleared ctDNA

models.name: GI102 sub‑protocol C (dose level 1B)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 40 pts, KRAS G12D/V/R, first‑line metastatic PDAC

MOA_summary: Deruxnaratib binds cyclophilin A and forms a complex that sterically blocks effector binding to GTP‑bound KRAS (codons 12,13,61 and WT), suppressing MAPK signaling; combined with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel, it yields high response rates and deep ctDNA reductions in KRAS‑mutant PDAC.
key_findings: Confirmed ORR of 58% and DCR of 90% in 40 first‑line PDAC patients receiving deruxnaratib + gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel.
Six‑month landmark PFS and OS were 84% and 90%, respectively, exceeding historical chemotherapy benchmarks.
96% of evaluable patients achieved ≥50% reduction in KRAS ctDNA; 61% cleared detectable KRAS mutation.
Grade ≥3 rash occurred in ~10‑15% of patients; overall safety profile was manageable with dose reductions.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard chemotherapy backbone to enhance tumor kill while KRAS inhibition blocks MAPK signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: High ORR and durable disease control in KRAS‑mutant PDAC.

open_questions: Will maintenance deruxnaratib after chemotherapy improve long‑term outcomes?
How does efficacy differ among KRAS alleles (G12D vs G12V vs G12R)?
